title: Major Grey's chutney
subtitle: Anglo-Indian mango chutney with raisins and ginger
excerpt: Ripe mango slow-cooked with raisins, ginger, garlic, vinegar, and sugar into a thick, jammy chutney with a warm spice note. The Anglo-Indian preserve that became the standard accompaniment to curries across Victorian Britain — sweet, sour, and mild.
sourceNotes: Based on the Anglo-Indian mango chutney tradition documented in Lizzie Collingham, 'Curry: A Tale of Cooks and Conquerors' (2005) and historical recipes for 'Major Grey's' style chutney, which became a commercial category in Britain from the mid-nineteenth century. The original commercial product was manufactured by Crosse and Blackwell from approximately 1870; the recipe here follows the traditional domestic preparation.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale freely. Pour into sterilised jars while hot and seal.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 3 months in a sealed sterilised jar in a cool dark place. Refrigerate once opened.

content.text: Major Grey's chutney is not a specific recipe but a style: a sweet, mild mango and raisin preserve with ginger, spiced mildly and finished with enough vinegar to give it a clean sharpness. It became the most commercially successful Anglo-Indian condiment ever made — the Crosse and Blackwell version was exported worldwide from the 1870s and for most British households of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries it was the only chutney they knew.

The method is straightforward: everything cooks together in one pot until the mango softens into the raisins and the liquid reduces to a thick, jammy consistency. The finished chutney should be spoonable rather than pourable. Use ripe mangoes for the sweetest result — under-ripe ones need significantly more sugar to balance the sharpness.

items.prepNote: ripe; peeled and diced (about 2 large mangoes)

content.text: Combine {{mango}} of diced ripe mango, {{raisins}} of raisins, {{caster-sugar}} of sugar, {{white-wine-vinegar}} of white wine vinegar, {{ginger-fresh}} grated ginger, {{garlic}} crushed garlic, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a large saucepan. Stir over a medium heat until the sugar dissolves. Bring to a gentle boil, then reduce to a steady simmer.

Cook uncovered for 35–40 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes, until the mango has broken down, the raisins are plump, and the mixture is thick enough to leave a clean path when a spoon is dragged across the bottom of the pan. Stir in {{garam-masala}} of garam masala. Taste and adjust salt and vinegar. Pour into a sterilised jar and seal immediately if preserving for storage, or leave to cool and refrigerate for shorter-term use.

items.cause: Very ripe mangoes with high natural sugar, or sugar quantity too high
items.fix: Add another tablespoon of vinegar and taste. The balance should be sweet-sour, not cloying.

items.symptom: Chutney is too thin after 40 minutes
items.cause: Very juicy mangoes releasing more liquid than expected
items.fix: Continue cooking uncovered. The setting point is reached when a drop on a cold plate holds its shape rather than spreading.

items.symptom: Chutney catches and burns on the bottom
items.cause: Heat too high or insufficient stirring as it thickens
items.fix: Reduce the heat and stir more frequently once the chutney has thickened past the halfway point.

content.text: The legend of Major Grey, the British officer in colonial India who is said to have created the recipe, is almost certainly apocryphal, a marketing invention of the kind common to Victorian food products. What is certain is that mango chutneys of this style were made in Anglo-Indian households throughout the nineteenth century and exported to Britain as bottled condiments, where they became the standard accompaniment to cold meats, cheese, and the curries that Anglo-Indian households introduced to British cooking. The name became a generic trademark in the United States; in Britain it describes a style rather than a specific product.
